skill.name: sql-injection-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized SQL injection assessment by explaining detection, exploitation evidence, database fingerprinting, and remediation reporting boundaries.
skill.summary: Structured guidance for authorized SQL injection vulnerability assessment and reporting.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static external-command alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and SQL examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. However, the skill provides detailed SQL injection exploitation, authentication bypass, credential extraction, out-of-band exfiltration, and filter-evasion guidance, creating high dual-use publication risk.

high_findings.title: Detailed SQL Injection Exploitation Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill moves beyond detection into UNION-based extraction, error-based extraction, blind extraction, and time-based extraction. These instructions can enable unauthorized database access when used outside an approved test.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section gives concrete exploitation workflows and data extraction payload categories. The surrounding purpose text confirms the skill is intended to demonstrate exploitation techniques.

high_findings.title: Out-of-Band Data Exfiltration Examples
high_findings.description: The skill includes DNS and HTTP out-of-band extraction patterns using attacker-controlled domains. This is a high-risk technique because it can move database data to external infrastructure.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly describes exfiltrating data through external channels and shows attacker-domain examples. This directly confirms the static hardcoded URL network concern.

high_findings.title: Authentication Bypass and Credential Exposure Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill provides login bypass payloads and examples that display administrator credentials. This can facilitate unauthorized account access if used without strict authorization.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe bypassing password checks and displaying admin credentials. The risk is semantic and does not depend on executable code being present.

high_findings.title: Filter and WAF Evasion Techniques
high_findings.description: The skill teaches encoding, comment insertion, keyword splitting, double writing, and null-byte patterns to bypass filtering. This reduces defensive controls and raises misuse risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly titled as bypass guidance and lists multiple evasion methods. Legitimate testing is possible, but the operational content is directly usable for misuse.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code block delimiters and SQL payload examples. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command runner was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown and the flagged backticks delimit examples. I found no runtime code path that executes shell commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings do not identify a cryptographic implementation. The cited content is skill metadata or SQL testing guidance rather than hashing or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive text and operational guardrails, not cryptographic algorithms. No evidence of weak hashing or encryption usage was found.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Alert Reflects Required Test Target
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance alert maps to prerequisites for an authorized web application target. This is expected for a penetration testing skill, but it reinforces the need for authorization controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines require a target URL and proxy tools, which is normal for web security testing. The same capability can be misused, so the finding is not fully dismissible.

dangerous_patterns.title: SQL Injection Payload Catalog
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains ready-to-use SQL injection payload patterns for boolean, UNION, error, time-based, and authentication-bypass testing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content directly lists SQL injection payload categories and examples. The risk is clear from the documented testing and bypass workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Exfiltration Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows payloads that route database-derived values through attacker-controlled DNS or HTTP destinations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly names out-of-band extraction and uses attacker-controlled domains. This is direct evidence of an exfiltration technique.

content.value_statement: SQL injection testing can expose sensitive systems if it is unstructured or unauthorized. This skill organizes approved testing steps, evidence collection, and remediation reporting for controlled security assessments.
actual_capabilities: Explains common SQL injection detection checks for parameters, forms, cookies, and headers.
Outlines UNION, error-based, boolean blind, and time-based SQL injection techniques.
Describes database fingerprinting concepts across MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle.
Provides guidance for documenting evidence, screenshots, request logs, and sanitized database findings.
Includes operational guardrails for written authorization, test scope, and data handling.
limitations: It does not verify that a user has legal authorization for the target.
It does not automatically patch vulnerable application code.
Its exploitation examples are high risk and require strict scope control.
It may be blocked by WAF, IPS, rate limits, or parameterized queries.

use_cases.title: Authorized Web App Assessment
use_cases.description: Plan a scoped SQL injection assessment and collect evidence without exceeding the written test agreement.

use_cases.title: Developer Remediation Review
use_cases.description: Understand how SQL injection flaws are detected so vulnerable query patterns can be replaced with safer database access.

use_cases.title: Security Report Preparation
use_cases.description: Organize vulnerability impact, proof of concept evidence, and remediation guidance for a client report.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Safe Test Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a SQL injection test plan for my authorized target. Include scope checks, evidence rules, stop conditions, and safe reporting steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review Injection Indicators
prompt_templates.prompt: I observed different responses from true and false SQL conditions on an in-scope parameter. Help me classify the evidence and avoid over-testing.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Remediation Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to remediate a confirmed SQL injection flaw in a login query. Focus on parameterized queries, validation, least privilege, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Executive Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my authorized SQL injection evidence into an executive-ready finding with impact, likelihood, affected assets, and remediation priorities.

output_examples.input: User asks for a controlled SQL injection assessment plan for a scoped staging application.
output_examples.output: A phased plan covering authorization checks, parameter inventory, safe detection steps, evidence handling, stop conditions, and report sections.

output_examples.input: User provides evidence of a login bypass in an approved test environment.
output_examples.output: A concise finding summary with affected function, business impact, reproduction notes, remediation actions, and validation criteria.

output_examples.input: Developer asks how to prevent similar SQL injection flaws.
output_examples.output: Practical remediation guidance covering parameterized queries, input validation, database permissions, error handling, and regression tests.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, and emergency contacts before any testing begins.
Minimize extracted data and sanitize all evidence before storing or sharing it.
Prioritize remediation with parameterized queries, least privilege, and regression tests.
anti_patterns: Do not test public or third-party systems without explicit written permission.
Do not extract full databases when a minimal proof of concept is sufficient.
Do not use evasion techniques to bypass defensive controls outside the approved scope.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: No. The audit rates it high risk because it includes detailed exploitation and exfiltration guidance.

faq.question: Does the skill contain executable malware?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file is Markdown guidance, not executable code.

faq.question: Why were backticks flagged by static analysis?
faq.answer: They appear to be Markdown code fences and payload examples, not Ruby or shell execution.

faq.question: Who is the intended user?
faq.answer: The content is intended for authorized penetration testers, security consultants, and developers fixing SQL injection flaws.

faq.question: What controls are required before use?
faq.answer: Users need written authorization, defined scope, data handling rules, and clear stop conditions.

faq.question: Can this skill remediate vulnerabilities automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can explain remediation practices, but developers must update and test the application code.
